passage: When Government Secondary School, Ipetu, reopened its library after many years of neglect, most students went there at first out of curiosity. The shelves were newly painted, the windows were wider than before, and a reading corner had been furnished with simple wooden chairs. Yet the principal knew that paint alone could not produce readers. She therefore introduced a weekly quiet-reading period during which every class visited the library with a teacher. At the beginning, some students only flipped through magazines, while others whispered and laughed. After a month, however, many began to borrow storybooks and science texts. The English teachers observed that students who read more regularly expressed themselves with greater confidence in class. Even in subjects like Government and Biology, answers became fuller and clearer. By the end of the term, the library was no longer seen as a decorated room but as a workshop of the mind. The principal then appealed to parents to donate old but useful books so that the improvement would not fade.
question: According to the passage, what practical step did the principal introduce to encourage reading?

options.A: A daily test in the library
options.B: A weekly quiet-reading period
options.D: A competition for only final-year students

answer_text: A weekly quiet-reading period

passage: When Government Secondary School, Ipetu, reopened its library after many years of neglect, most students went there at first out of curiosity. The shelves were newly painted, the windows were wider than before, and a reading corner had been furnished with simple wooden chairs. Yet the principal knew that paint alone could not produce readers. She therefore introduced a weekly quiet-reading period during which every class visited the library with a teacher. At the beginning, some students only flipped through magazines, while others whispered and laughed. After a month, however, many began to borrow storybooks and science texts. The English teachers observed that students who read more regularly expressed themselves with greater confidence in class. Even in subjects like Government and Biology, answers became fuller and clearer. By the end of the term, the library was no longer seen as a decorated room but as a workshop of the mind. The principal then appealed to parents to donate old but useful books so that the improvement would not fade.

passage: In Kwasau village, farmers used to lose a large part of their tomato harvest during the peak rainy season. The fruits spoiled quickly before they could be sold or transported to town. Last year, a youth group, working with an agricultural extension officer, built a simple solar dryer from wood, wire mesh and transparent plastic sheets. At first, many villagers doubted that such a modest structure could make much difference. But after several weeks of use, sliced tomatoes dried more evenly and stayed usable for far longer than those spread on mats in the open. The dryer also protected the food from dust, insects and sudden showers. Women who processed the tomatoes discovered that they could now sell part of the produce weeks after harvest, when prices were better. Encouraged by this success, the village council asked the youth group to train more households. The project did not remove every farming problem, but it showed that practical local ideas can reduce waste, increase income and strengthen confidence in community effort.

options.B: Much of their tomato harvest spoiled quickly
options.D: They had no buyers in the village

answer_text: Much of their tomato harvest spoiled quickly

passage: For years, residents of Arowolo Street depended on a shallow stream that often turned brown after heavy rain. During the dry season, the stream shrank so badly that children had to wake before dawn to fetch enough water for household use. Complaints were common, but little changed until a traders' association and the residents' committee agreed to contribute money for a borehole. The work was not smooth. Some people argued over the site, while others delayed payment. In spite of these setbacks, drilling was completed before the next dry season. To prevent the common fate of abandoned public projects, the community appointed two caretakers and created a small maintenance levy. At first, a few residents grumbled about the levy, but they later realized that repairing a faulty pump would cost much more if no fund existed. The borehole did more than supply cleaner water. It reduced the time spent on water collection, improved punctuality among schoolchildren and showed that shared problems can be solved when people are willing to contribute and protect what they build together.

passage: For years, residents of Arowolo Street depended on a shallow stream that often turned brown after heavy rain. During the dry season, the stream shrank so badly that children had to wake before dawn to fetch enough water for household use. Complaints were common, but little changed until a traders' association and the residents' committee agreed to contribute money for a borehole. The work was not smooth. Some people argued over the site, while others delayed payment. In spite of these setbacks, drilling was completed before the next dry season. To prevent the common fate of abandoned public projects, the community appointed two caretakers and created a small maintenance levy. At first, a few residents grumbled about the levy, but they later realized that repairing a faulty pump would cost much more if no fund existed. The borehole did more than supply cleaner water. It reduced the time spent on water collection, improved punctuality among schoolchildren and showed that shared problems can be solved when people are willing to contribute and protect what they build together.
question: Which statement is true according to the passage?

options.A: The drilling was completed after the dry season
options.B: Everyone paid immediately and happily
options.C: Some people argued over where to locate the borehole
options.D: The stream remained clear after heavy rain

answer_text: Some people argued over where to locate the borehole

passage: For years, residents of Arowolo Street depended on a shallow stream that often turned brown after heavy rain. During the dry season, the stream shrank so badly that children had to wake before dawn to fetch enough water for household use. Complaints were common, but little changed until a traders' association and the residents' committee agreed to contribute money for a borehole. The work was not smooth. Some people argued over the site, while others delayed payment. In spite of these setbacks, drilling was completed before the next dry season. To prevent the common fate of abandoned public projects, the community appointed two caretakers and created a small maintenance levy. At first, a few residents grumbled about the levy, but they later realized that repairing a faulty pump would cost much more if no fund existed. The borehole did more than supply cleaner water. It reduced the time spent on water collection, improved punctuality among schoolchildren and showed that shared problems can be solved when people are willing to contribute and protect what they build together.

options.A: The stream shrank during the dry season

answer_text: The borehole did more than supply cleaner water

passage: As examinations approached, Tunde formed the habit of reading far into the night. At first, he felt proud of his new discipline. He drank strong coffee, copied pages of notes and boasted to his friends that sleep was a waste of precious time. For a few days, his routine appeared effective. Soon, however, he began to discover its hidden cost. In class, his eyes grew heavy and his attention drifted. He found it harder to remember formulas he had memorized at midnight. During one test, he misread two instructions, not because the questions were difficult, but because his mind was dull. His class teacher later explained that rest is not an enemy of work. Sleep allows the brain to recover and helps memory settle what has been studied. Tunde adjusted his timetable, revised earlier in the evening and slept for reasonable hours. Although he now spent fewer hours at his desk, he understood more, forgot less and became calmer during tests. He learned that successful study depends not only on effort, but also on wise habits.

passage: At Oja-Oke market, many women sold pepper, garri and beans in very small quantities. Because each trader bought goods alone, transport costs were high and profits remained thin. Matters changed when a retired teacher helped them form a cooperative society. Under the arrangement, members contributed weekly and bought goods in bulk from wholesalers. This reduced the cost per bag and gave them stronger bargaining power. The cooperative also kept simple records of savings and loans. Some members who had never entered a bank before learned to calculate repayment dates and to separate business money from household spending. Not every difficulty vanished. A few members missed meetings, and one woman complained that the record book was too strict. Yet after six months, most traders admitted that the discipline had protected them from careless borrowing and wasteful spending. Their stalls were better stocked, quarrels with middlemen reduced, and several women could now pay school fees more easily. In a market where many had worked hard for years without noticeable progress, cooperation turned scattered effort into measurable growth.

passage: At Oja-Oke market, many women sold pepper, garri and beans in very small quantities. Because each trader bought goods alone, transport costs were high and profits remained thin. Matters changed when a retired teacher helped them form a cooperative society. Under the arrangement, members contributed weekly and bought goods in bulk from wholesalers. This reduced the cost per bag and gave them stronger bargaining power. The cooperative also kept simple records of savings and loans. Some members who had never entered a bank before learned to calculate repayment dates and to separate business money from household spending. Not every difficulty vanished. A few members missed meetings, and one woman complained that the record book was too strict. Yet after six months, most traders admitted that the discipline had protected them from careless borrowing and wasteful spending. Their stalls were better stocked, quarrels with middlemen reduced, and several women could now pay school fees more easily. In a market where many had worked hard for years without noticeable progress, cooperation turned scattered effort into measurable growth.

passage: At Oja-Oke market, many women sold pepper, garri and beans in very small quantities. Because each trader bought goods alone, transport costs were high and profits remained thin. Matters changed when a retired teacher helped them form a cooperative society. Under the arrangement, members contributed weekly and bought goods in bulk from wholesalers. This reduced the cost per bag and gave them stronger bargaining power. The cooperative also kept simple records of savings and loans. Some members who had never entered a bank before learned to calculate repayment dates and to separate business money from household spending. Not every difficulty vanished. A few members missed meetings, and one woman complained that the record book was too strict. Yet after six months, most traders admitted that the discipline had protected them from careless borrowing and wasteful spending. Their stalls were better stocked, quarrels with middlemen reduced, and several women could now pay school fees more easily. In a market where many had worked hard for years without noticeable progress, cooperation turned scattered effort into measurable growth.
question: A suitable title for the passage is

options.B: The Growth of a Traders' Cooperative
